title: Decision on the merits: European Federation of National Organisations working with the Homeless (FEANTSA) and International Federation for Human Rights (FIDH) v. France, Complaint No. 224/2023
text: 2 European Committee of Social Rights Comité européen des Droits sociaux DECISION ON THE MERITS Adoption: 15 October 2025 Notification: 4 November 2025 Publication: 5 March 2026 European Federation of National Organisations working with the Homeless (FEANTSA) and International Federation for Human Rights (FIDH) v. France Complaint No. 224/2023 The European Committee of Social Rights, committee of independent experts (“the Committee”) established under Article 25 of the European Social Charter, during its 351st session in the following composition: Tatiana PUIU, Vice-President George THEODOSIS, Vice-President Kristine DUPATE, General Rapporteur Karin Møhl LARSEN Yusuf BALCI Mario VINKOVIĆ Miriam KULLMANN Carmen SALCEDO BELTRÁN Franz MARHOLD Alla FEDOROVA Olivier DE SCHUTTER Kristina KOLDINSKÁ Carmen-Constantina NENU Assisted by Henrik KRISTENSEN, Executive Secretary Having deliberated on 10 September and 15 October 2025, On the basis of the report presented by Tatiana PUIU, Delivers the following decision, adopted on the latter date: PROCEDURE 1. The complaint lodged by the European Federation of National Organisations working with the Homeless (FEANTSA) and International Federation for Human Rights (FIDH) (“the complainant organisations”) was registered on 3 April 2023. 2. The complainant organisations allege that the absence of specific legal or regulatory provisions designed to circumscribe the proliferation in France of municipal decrees aimed at punishing behaviour generally adopted by people living in poverty in public spaces, together with the imprecision of the criteria established by domestic case law, demonstrate that the national legal framework does not protect people living in poverty and constitutes a violation of Articles 11, 30 and 31, as well as Article E read in conjunction with the aforementioned provisions of the revised European Social Charter (“the Charter”). 3. On 17 October 2023, the Committee declared the complaint admissible. 4. Referring to Article 7§1 of the 1995 Protocol Providing for a System of Collective Complaints ("the Protocol"), the Committee asked the Government to submit written submissions on the merits of the complaint before 15 December 2023. 5. Referring to Article 7§§1, 2 of the Protocol and in application of Article 32§§1, 2 of its Rules of Procedure ("the Rules"), the Committee invited the States Parties to the Protocol, the States having made a declaration in accordance with Article D§2 of the Charter, as well as the international organisations of employers or trade unions referred to in Article 27§2 of the 1961 Charter, if they so wished, to submit observations on the merits of the complaint before 15 December 2023. 6. On 15 December 2023, the Government requested an extension of the deadline for presenting its submissions on the merits of the complaint. The President of the Committee granted an extension until 5 January 2024. 7. The Government's submissions on the merits of the complaint were registered on 5 January 2024. In accordance with Rule 31§2 of the Rules, the complainant organisations were invited to submit a response to the Government's submissions by 15 March 2024. 8. The complainant organisations’ response was registered on 15 March 2024. 9. In accordance with Rule 31§3 of the Rules, the Government was invited to reply to the complainant organisations’ response by 15 May 2024. 10. The reply from the Government was registered on 15 May 2024. SUBMISSIONS OF THE PARTIES A – The complainant organisations 11. According to the complainant organisations, there has been a proliferation of municipal decrees across France that prohibit and penalise life-sustaining conduct and conduct inherently linked to poverty and homelessness, such as begging, occupying public spaces for prolonged periods, congregating in groups with or without dogs, misusing communal facilities, consuming alcohol, playing music loudly, or urinating in public. The complainant organisations submit that these measures are grounded in prejudice against persons living in poverty and that their systematic enforcement intensifies those persons’ social exclusion. They accordingly request the Committee to find that the situation in France is in violation of Articles 11, 30, and 31§1 alone and Article E read in conjunction with the aforementioned provisions of the Charter. B – The respondent Government 12. The Government rejects the allegations of the complainant organisations and asks the Committee to find that there is no violation of Articles 11, 30, 31§1, and E of the Charter with respect to the French legislative and regulatory framework governing the police powers of mayors. RELEVANT DOMESTIC LAW AND PRACTICE A – Legislation 13. Mayors issue decrees (“arrêtés”) pursuant to their police powers to protect public order, safety, security, and public health (hereafter referred to as “municipal decrees”). The relevant provisions of the General Code on Local and Regional Authorities read as follows: Art. L. 2212-1 The mayor shall be responsible, under the administrative supervision of the state’s representative in the département, for the municipal police, the rural police and the execution of acts of the state relating to them. Art. L. 2212-2 The purpose of the municipal police is to guarantee order, safety, security and public health. Its tasks include: 1° Everything relating to safe and easy passage on streets, quays, squares and public thoroughfares, including cleaning, lighting, removal of encumbrances, demolition or repair of buildings in danger of collapse, prohibition on placing anything at windows or other parts of buildings which may cause damage if it falls and on throwing anything which may harm or injure passers-by or cause harmful fumes; 2° Punishment of disturbances of the peace, such as fights and arguments accompanied by riots in the streets, commotion brought about in places of public assembly, mobs, noise, including neighbourhood noise, night-time gatherings preventing residents from resting and any other act liable to disturb the peace; 3° Preserving law and order in places and settings in which where there are large gatherings of people, such as fairs, markets, public entertainments and ceremonies, performances, games, cafés, churches and other public places; […] 7° Preventing or remedying any untoward events which may be caused by the unchecked roaming of harmful or aggressive animals; […] Since 2022, the breach of a prohibition set out in a municipal decree has been classified as a contravention of the 2nd class (Art. R. 610-5 of the Criminal Code), punishable by a €150 fine (Art. 131-13 of the Criminal Code). 14. Article L. 2131-8 of the General Code on Local and Regional Authorities provides that the State representative in the département (i.e., the prefect) or any “aggrieved natural or legal person” has standing to challenge municipal decrees before administrative courts. In the case of municipal decrees concerning begging, the latter category includes national non-governmental organisations on the grounds that “[their appeals] raise, by reason of their effects, in particular in the field of civil liberties, questions which, by their nature and purpose, go beyond local circumstances alone” (CE, 4 November 2015, no. 375178, § 2). Notably, prefects must receive notification of all municipal decrees, enabling them to exercise their right of appeal where appropriate. 15. While France decriminalised “begging” (“la mendicité”) and “wandering” (“le vagabondage”) in 1994, the Criminal Code retains the following offences: - “exploitation of begging” (“l'exploitation de la mendicité”), with aggravated circumstances when committed against a minor or other vulnerable person, by a person in a position of authority, or in a group, among others (Arts. 225-12-5 - 225-12-6); - “extortion” (“la demande de fonds sous contrainte”) defined as “soliciting others to hand over money, valuables or any property on a public thoroughfare in a group in an aggressive manner, or with the threat of a dangerous animal” (Art. 312-12-1); - “endangering children’s health and morals” (“la mise en péril de la santé et de la moralité des mineurs”), defined as “keeping a child under six years of age on a public highway or in a place used for the purposes of public transport with the aim of soliciting the generosity of passers-by” (Art. 227-15); - “human trafficking” (“la traite des êtres humains”), including for the purposes of begging (Art. 225-4-1); - “[unlicensed] street vending” (“la vente à la sauvette”, Art. 446-1); - “failure to control a dangerous animal” (“de la divagation d'animaux dangereux”, Art. R622-2) ; - “nocturnal noises and disturbances” (“des bruits ou tapages injurieux ou nocturnes”, Art. R623-2). 16. The Transport Code prohibits begging in public railway facilities and on trains (Art. R. 2241-16), as well as in bus stations (Art. R. 3116-8). Article L. 4741-8 of the Labour Code prohibits “employing children for habitual begging, either openly or under the guise of a profession.” Article L. 3341-1 of the Public Health Code, on the “repression of public drunkenness” authorises law enforcement authorities to detain any person found drunk in public in a sobering cell. The corresponding regulation (Art. R. 3353-1) provides that being “in a manifest state of intoxication in public” is punishable by a contravention of the 2nd class, i.e. a fine up to €150. 17. Article L. 115-1 of the Social Action and Family Code states that all public policies shall pursue the objective of combating poverty and exclusion, based on respect for the equal dignity of all human beings, and ensuring effective access to fundamental rights in several areas, including employment and housing. Moreover, State agencies are required to pursue a policy aimed at identifying, preventing, and eliminating all situations that may result in poverty and exclusion. 18. Discrimination on the grounds of “economic vulnerability” (“la particulière vulnérabilité résultant de la situation économique, apparente ou connue de son auteur”) is defined as a criminal offence (Art. 225-1 of the Criminal Code) and is prohibited by the Labour Code (Art. L. 1132-1) under Law No. 2016-832 of 24 June 2016. Municipal decrees on begging 19. Local authorities may prohibit a range of behaviours by decree, including, primarily, begging, whether from a seated, lying, or standing position, and carried out in either a “passive” or an “aggressive” manner. They also commonly prohibit “excessive and prolonged occupation of public spaces”, often described as a means of preventing anti-social behaviour. Municipal decrees may further refer to the ‘appropriation’ of public space and prohibit behaviours such as misusing communal facilities such as benches or bus shelters, storing or setting up equipment of any type without a licence, stretching laundry, heating food, camping, congregating with others, or gatherings of dogs, even when on a leash. Some municipal decrees prohibit all forms of “disorderly” behaviour, typically in connection with alcohol consumption, such as playing music loudly, and making noise, littering, smoking in the proximity of schools, or urinating in public. They may also target informal labour activities in the public domain, such as street vending, street entertainment, washing car windshields or waste picking. 20. To comply with the requirements imposed by the Conseil d'État (see §§ 23-24 below), the scope of the prohibitions included in municipal decrees must be limited in time (i.e., up to several months each year, on certain days of the week, and during specific hourly intervals). Accordingly, local authorities may adopt similar decrees successively during the same year or on a yearly basis (e.g. the mayor of Melun adopted eight identical decrees between October 2021 and February 2024). The scope of the prohibitions in question is also commonly limited to certain well-defined geographic locations (e.g. a clearly defined urban perimeter, particular streets or squares, or areas in proximity to specific locations such as cash dispensers). 21. Municipal decrees invoke several considerations falling broadly within the scope of “public order” within the meaning of Article L. 2212-2 of the General Code on Local and Regional Authorities to justify the prohibition of begging or of other such conduct. These may include public safety; public health and hygiene; tranquillity and peaceful enjoyment of possessions by inhabitants; and unencumbered pedestrian passage; prevention of disorder and noise; free movement of vehicles; normal use of public facilities and public highways, including unimpeded access to local businesses. In addition, municipal decrees commonly refer to the need to preserve the local image - in view of a large influx of tourists, particularly during seasonal festivities and high tourist periods - as well as the narrowness of the streets in urban centres, which cannot be altered due to the need to preserve architectural heritage. Moreover, they typically cite the high number of complaints received from residents, traders, and passers-by as evidence of the need for regulatory intervention. B – Domestic case law 22. According to settled principle, administrative review is founded on the maxim that “freedom is the rule, and police restriction is the exception,” implying that mayors’ police powers are qualified (CE, 10 August 1917, n° 59855) and that absolute prohibitions are liable to be invalidated. It is commonly accepted that municipal decrees on begging affect the freedom of movement of those concerned (see for example, CE, 16 July 2021, no. 434254) or even “the freedom to help others for humanitarian purposes,” derived from the principle of solidarity, which has received constitutional status (Constitutional Council, 6 July 2018, no. 2018-717/718). 23. It also follows that administrative review entails assessing whether municipal decrees are necessary and proportionate to the aim of protecting public order. In doing so, administrative courts are guided by principles laid down by the Conseil d'État in a series of decisions, whereby any restrictions imposed by decree must have geographic, temporal, and material limitations. For example, in 2003, the Conseil d'État upheld the decision of a lower-instance court finding that a municipal decree on begging was proportionate to the aim pursued. The relevant part of the decision reads as follows: “[the decree] included provisions limited to the summer period and applicable only to certain roads in the centre of the town and to the surroundings of certain large stores, […] these measures did not exceed those that the mayor could legally enact to ensure preventively, during periods of tourist influx, the safety, convenience and tranquillity necessary for users of public roads and […] the restrictions imposed, taking into account their limitation in time and space, did not subject the persons concerned to excessive constraints other than those imposed by compliance with the objectives pursued.” (CE, 9 July 2003, n°229618) 24. Conversely, in a decision delivered in 2021, the Conseil d'État invalidated a municipal decree on begging on the grounds that: “The […] contested decree prohibits, as being of a nature to in itself disturb public order, the mere fact of leaving more than two dogs standing, even temporarily, on the public highway, as well as, in general, the fact that a group of more than three people emit noises of conversation and music "audible to passers-by", without specifying the duration or intensity. The measures thus enacted by the contested decree for a period of three months, without any time slot limitation and every day of the week, in a vast geographical area corresponding to the entire city centre of the municipality, must be regarded, even though the municipality of Saint-Etienne claims an increase in delinquency and incivility in its city centre, as constituting, due to the general and absolute nature of the prohibitions thus imposed, an infringement of personal freedom, in particular the freedom to come and go, which is disproportionate to the objective of safeguarding public order pursued.” (CE, 16 July 2021, n° 434254) RELEVANT INTERNATIONAL MATERIALS A – Council of Europe European Court of Human Rights case-law on begging laws 25. In Lacatus v. Switzerland, the applicant, a poor and vulnerable Roma woman, complained about the fine imposed on her for non-intrusive begging, and her subsequent five days’ imprisonment for non-payment. The European Court of Human Rights (ECtHR) highlighted the breadth of the regulations in question, which amounted to a blanket ban on begging, and narrowing the State’s margin of appreciation in this area (Lacatus v. Switzerland, no. 14065/15, § 105, 19 January 2021). In weighing the interests at stake, the ECtHR noted that, as the applicant “was from an extremely poor family, was illiterate, was unemployed, and was not in receipt of social benefits”, she “had the right, inherent in human dignity, to be able to convey her plight and attempt to meet her basic needs through begging” (ibid., § 107). In view of the severity of the sanction imposed, which was liable to “increase [her] distress and vulnerability” (ibid., §109), and the absence of other extenuating circumstances, the ECtHR concluded that there had been a violation of the right to respect for private life under Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) (ibid., § 117). 26. In Dian v. Denmark, the applicant, a 61-year-old, illiterate and unemployed Romanian national, complained about being convicted for begging and sentenced to twenty days’ imprisonment (Dian v. Denmark (dec.), no. 44002/22, 21 May 2024). The ECtHR distinguished this case from Lacatus on two grounds - the applicant’s social and economic situation, and the scope of the prohibition concerned. As to the former, it was determined that the applicant earned a living from selling newspapers, collecting bottles, and begging; that he was able to send money to his family in Romania where he also had a house, that he had been a cocaine and cannabis user for years; and that the police found him to be in possession of €135 in cash. In addition, as an EU citizen, he had the right to enter and reside in Denmark for up to three months and, in case of financial emergency, could have applied for “return assistance”. The ECtHR was therefore unable to conclude that begging had been the applicant’s only option to ensure his own survival. As to the latter, Denmark did not have a blanket ban on begging, which was allowed under certain conditions. A person could only be convicted if begging took place in a manner causing nuisance to the public and the person had been warned beforehand, or if it occurred in a pedestrian street, at stations, in or outside supermarkets or on public transport. Moreover, there was no indication that the applicant had been regularly convicted of begging or that he was prevented from begging in Denmark at all. Accordingly, the ECtHR concluded that Article 8 was not applicable to the facts and rejected the complaint as inadmissible. European Court of Human Rights case-law on the right to housing 27. Multiple judgments against France illustrate a range of regulatory and operational failures that resulted in vulnerable applicants becoming or remaining homeless, as follows. The refusal of the administrative authorities to enforce orders by the urgent-applications judge requiring the State to provide several asylum seekers and their children with emergency accommodation constituted a violation of the right to a fair trial under Article 6§1 of the ECHR (M.K. and Others v. France, nos. 34349/18 and 2 others, 8 December 2022; S.A. and Others v. France, nos. 40429/19 and 53466/21, 23 November 2023). The failure to comply with a court order requiring the applicant’s urgent rehousing for over three years after she had been deemed eligible under the law on the enforceable right to housing (known as the “DALO Act”) was in violation of Article 6§1 (Tchokontio Happi v. France, no. 65829/12, 9 April 2015). The authorities' failure to provide a 12-year-old unaccompanied minor with care and protection, before and after the dismantling of the southern zone of the camp in Calais known as the “Jungle,” due to his particularly poor living conditions and the non-enforcement of a placement order of the juvenile judge aimed at protecting him, amounted to a violation of the prohibition of degrading treatment under Article 3 of the ECHR (Khan v. France, no. 12267/16, 28 February 2019). The eviction of Roma and/or Travellers due to unlawful occupation without sufficient consideration being given to the needs of those families in that context, including with respect to housing, amounted to a violation of the right to respect for private and family life and the home under Article 8 of the ECHR (Winterstein and Others v. France, no. 27013/07, 17 October 2013; Hirtu and Others v. France, no. 24720/13, 14 May 2020). The Convention on Action against Trafficking in Human Beings 28. The Convention on Action against Trafficking in Human Beings entered into force on 1 February 2008 and was ratified by France on 1 May 2008. Article 1 of the Convention reads as follows: Article 1 – Purposes of the Convention “1. The purposes of this Convention are: a. to prevent and combat trafficking in human beings, while guaranteeing gender equality; b. to protect the human rights of the victims of trafficking, design a comprehensive framework for the protection and assistance of victims and witnesses, while guaranteeing gender equality, as well as to ensure effective investigation and prosecution; […]” Parliamentary Assembly, Recommendation 2003 (2012) on Roma migrants in Europe 29. In this Recommendation, the Parliamentary Assembly emphasised that prejudice, combined with the widespread tendency to make a generalised link between Roma and criminality, had contributed greatly to the plight of Roma in Europe. Concerning the criminalisation of begging, it recommended that the Committee of Ministers instruct the relevant Council of Europe committees and bodies to: “analyse legislation and practices in member States aimed at criminalising begging and evaluate the impact of this on Roma and the implications under the European Convention on Human Rights, the revised European Social Charter and other Council of Europe standards” (§ 6.1). European Commission against Racism and Intolerance (ECRI), Report on France (sixth monitoring cycle), 28 June 2022 30. In this report, ECRI made the following remarks as regards the housing situation of migrants in France: “89. [ECRI] notes with concern that the access to housing [of refugees] remains problematic in view of the difficulties in implementing the DALO Act (on the enforceable right to housing) and the fact that housing supply does not meet needs, which results in refugees all too often finding themselves on the streets, in squalid settlements on the outskirts of large cities. Similar or even greater difficulties affect asylum-seekers waiting for their applications to be processed or those whose applications have been refused and who are waiting to be removed. Lastly, ECRI regrets that shelter for vulnerable persons, including unaccompanied minors, was not always provided during the health crisis and is still problematic. It considers that housing policies should take these realities into account and calls on the authorities to take appropriate steps to improve the accommodation conditions of migrants, asylum-seekers and people granted refugee status or subsidiary protection. 31. ECRI also noted the high number of evictions of Travellers from unauthorised halting sites, often without any offers of alternative accommodation (§ 96). Roma were also frequently subject to evictions, as part of the authorities’ objective of “clearing illegal settlements” in order to integrate the communities living in them through access to health, housing, employment and schooling for their children (§ 100). In this context, ECRI expressed concern about the fate of people who had been evicted without being rehoused. Commissioner for Human Rights 32. In his 2015 report on France, Nils Muižnieks, then Commissioner for Human Rights, considered the ban on begging in Marseille to be an example of “anti-Gypsyism” (Report following a visit to France from 22 to 26 September 2014 (2015), § 171). Concerning Norway, he found that the blanket ban on non-aggressive begging had a discriminatory impact on Roma immigrants and interfered with their freedom of expression, concluding that such laws should be repealed (Report following a visit to Norway from 19 to 23 January 2015, §§ 59-67). Thematic report on legislation and policies related to begging, with special focus on children (2022) 33. This report, by the Committee of Experts on Roma and Traveller Issues (ADI-ROM), highlighted a very worrying trend of re-emerging anti-begging, anti-vagrancy and anti-trespass legislation increasingly being used across Europe to control the mobility of Roma and Travellers. The report asserted that trafficking in human beings specifically for the purpose of forced begging remained rather low and that families begging on the street were far more likely doing it because of their extreme poverty and marginalisation. As regards France, the report noted statistics showing that only about 3% of children begging were victims of child trafficking. 34. The report concluded that effective solutions to abolish the phenomena of child begging in Europe relied on a mid and long-term multi-pronged strategy. Developing suitable living conditions and ensuring access to kindergartens could rapidly decrease the rates of child begging. Improving access to education, employment, and social and health services and benefits could lift Roma communities out of extreme poverty and, in the long term, eliminate child begging entirely. The report asserted that such measures were preferable to an approach that resulted in Roma families simply moving on or emigrating temporarily out of the country, which tended to shift the problem but did not address or deter any of the root causes of child begging, negative societal effects, nor impacted trafficking in human beings. B – United Nations Special Rapporteur on adequate housing, Report on the visit to France, 28 August 2020 35. The Special Rapporteur summarised her findings from the visit as follows: “90. […] Housing affordability has become a significant problem, with housing prices and rental costs on the rise. There is an insufficient supply of social housing in metropolitan areas for those in need. Those in informal settlements are often denied basic services and regularly subjected to forced eviction. Homelessness is increasing at a significant pace as an acute shortage in emergency shelters and longer-term housing deepens. Migrants fleeing civil conflict or extreme poverty, who are living in some of the most egregious conditions, have put additional strain on national and local governments to ensure adequate and affordable housing for all. This situation, which includes barriers to access to justice and the opening of the housing market to private investors, has resulted in a housing sector under siege, despite Government efforts. […]” Committee on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights, Concluding observations on the fifth periodic report of France, 30 October 2023 36. Insofar as relevant to the present complaint, the Committee on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (‘the ESCR Committee’) noted that, despite the measures adopted by the State party, the incidence of poverty among certain disadvantaged and marginalized groups continued to worsen their situation of social exclusion, and that the poverty rate had not fallen, and was even rising slightly (§ 38). The ESCR Committee expressed further concern as regards the insufficient affordable housing and social housing, and insufficient emergency accommodation capacity to meet the needs of the most disadvantaged and marginalized groups; the high numbers of homeless persons and persons living in informal settlements in deplorable living conditions, particularly Roma; the deplorable living conditions of migrants and asylum-seekers in informal settlements, without access to water, sanitation, adequate food and health care; the forced evictions from informal settlements, often carried out without offering alternative housing solutions or taking into account the specific needs of the people concerned, particularly children (§ 42). Committee on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights, General comment No. 20 (2009) on non-discrimination in economic, social and cultural rights 37. The ESCR Committee includes socio-economic status among the implied grounds covered by the prohibition of discrimination under Article 2§2 of the International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights, based on the following considerations: “35. Individuals and groups of individuals must not be arbitrarily treated on account of belonging to a certain economic or social group or strata within society. A person’s social and economic situation when living in poverty or being homeless may result in pervasive discrimination, stigmatization and negative stereotyping which can lead to the refusal of, or unequal access to, the same quality of education and health care as others, as well as the denial of or unequal access to public places.” Special Rapporteur on extreme poverty and human rights, Report on the penalization of people living in poverty (2011) 38. This report contains an analysis of laws, regulations and practices that punish, segregate, control and undermine the autonomy of persons living in poverty. The report noted that such measures had been adopted with increasing frequency over the previous three decades, owing also to economic and financial crises, and that they represented a serious threat to the enjoyment of human rights by persons living in poverty. Human Rights Council, Guiding Principles on Extreme Poverty and Human Rights (2012) 39. The Guiding Principles on Extreme Poverty and Human Rights are referred to as the first global policy guidelines focused specifically on the human rights of people living in poverty. The Guiding Principles note inter alia that “fines imposed on persons living in poverty have a disproportionate impact on them, worsen their situation and perpetuate the vicious circle of poverty” and that “homeless persons in particular are frequently subject to restrictions on their freedom of movement and criminalized for using public space.” Consequently, “States should repeal or reform any laws that criminalize life-sustaining activities in public places, such as sleeping, begging, eating or performing personal hygiene activities.” 40. The Committee noted that the Guiding Principles, which reaffirm the human rights-based approach to poverty, constitute an important source of interpretation for Article 30 of the Charter (Conclusions 2013, Statement of interpretation on Article 30). Special Rapporteur on the right to adequate housing, Guidelines for the Implementation of the Right to Adequate Housing (2019) 41. In this document, the Special Rapporteur on the right to adequate housing outlines the key elements needed for the effective implementation of the right to housing. Guideline no. 5 refers to the requirement to eliminate homelessness in the shortest possible time and stop the criminalization of persons living in homelessness. Accordingly, States should prohibit and address discrimination on the ground of homelessness or other housing status and repeal all laws and measures that criminalize or penalize homeless people or behaviour associated with being homeless, such as sleeping or eating in public spaces. The forced eviction of homeless persons from public spaces and the destruction of their personal belongings must be prohibited. Special Rapporteur on the right to adequate housing and the Special Rapporteur on extreme poverty and human rights, Breaking the cycle: Ending the criminalization of homelessness and poverty (2024) 42. This joint study by the Special Rapporteur on the right to adequate housing and the Special Rapporteur on extreme poverty and human rights explains why the criminalization of persons experiencing homelessness or poverty is not a suitable solution and may violate a wide range of human rights. The study argues that criminalization perpetuates historical legacies of discrimination and exclusion, entrenches discrimination and stigmatization and is an unsuitable, inefficient and costly approach to address social issues. The study calls on States and local governments to repeal vagrancy laws and legislation penalizing life-sustaining activities in public spaces, such as sleeping, eating, begging or undertaking informal economic activities in public spaces. International Labour Organization (ILO), Violence and Harassment Convention 2019 (No. 190) 43. Convention No. 190 requires States to take appropriate measures to prevent violence and harassment in the world of work, including for informal economy workers, and to identify sectors, occupations and work arrangements in which workers are more exposed to violence and harassment, and take effective measures to protect such persons (Arts. 3 and 8). Article 8 of the Convention addresses the fact that many informal economy workers, particularly those who work in public spaces such as street vendors and waste pickers, face violence and harassment from public authorities in the form of the confiscation of goods, rape or sexual assault, or forceful dispersion (ILO, Violence and harassment in the world of work: A guide on Convention No. 190 and Recommendation No. 206, p. 44). C – European Union European Pillar of Social Rights 44. Principle 19 of the European Pillar of Social Rights reads as follows: “19. Housing and assistance for the homeless Access to social housing or housing assistance of good quality shall be provided for those in need. Vulnerable people have the right to appropriate assistance and protection against forced eviction. Adequate shelter and services shall be provided to the homeless in order to promote their social inclusion.” D – Other regional human rights mechanisms African Commission on Human and Peoples’ Rights, Principles on the Decriminalisation of Petty Offences in Africa (2017) 45. In adopting these principles, the African Commission declared that laws which created petty offences, including begging, were inconsistent with the principles of equality before the law and non-discrimination on the basis that they targeted, or had a disproportionate impact on, the poor and other vulnerable persons (§ 6). It added the following: “7. The enforcement of petty offences has the effect of punishing, segregating, controlling and undermining the dignity of persons on the basis of their status. They also infringe upon the autonomy of persons by restricting their performance of life-sustaining activities in public spaces, particularly for those living in poverty. The enforcement of these laws perpetuates the stigmatisation of poverty by mandating a criminal-justice response to what are socio-economic and sustainable development issues. In this regard, petty offences reinforce discriminatory attitudes against marginalised persons.” African Court on Human and Peoples’ Rights, Advisory Opinion of 4 December 2020 46. The African Court found that vagrancy laws, which “punish the poor and underprivileged, including (…) the homeless,” violated the prohibition on cruel, inhuman or degrading punishment and treatment. Moreover, the enforcement of vagrancy laws was contrary to the States’ obligation to protect the rights of women, children, and persons with disabilities. Inter-American Commission on Human Rights, Report on Poverty and Human Rights in the Americas (2017) 47. In this report, the Inter-American Commission on Human Rights emphasised that rules and practices restricting undesirable conduct and activities considered “undesirable” or contrary to public order, such as begging, sleeping or loitering in the streets, aggravated the situation of exclusion, disadvantage and discrimination faced by persons living in poverty (§ 177). Accordingly, the Commission stressed that the prohibition on begging and related activities could amount to a violation of the principles of equality and non-discrimination (§ 178). THE LAW PRELIMINARY CONSIDERATIONS 48. The complainant organisations submit that there has been a proliferation of municipal decrees that prohibit and punish life-sustaining conduct typically resorted to by persons living in poverty in public spaces – most notably begging, but also sitting, lying, sleeping, maintaining a prolonged presence, or gathering in groups. They allege that such measures are widespread and continue to be adopted across the country. The complainant organisations maintain that these measures are grounded in prejudice against persons living in poverty and that their systematic enforcement exacerbates their exclusion from public spaces and contributes to broader social exclusion. They contest the Government’s characterisation of the decrees as pursuing public-order objectives, asserting that they target the mere presence of homeless persons and others in situations of hardship in public spaces. 49. For its part, the Government submits that the powers of mayors to adopt such decrees are circumscribed by law, that they pursue legitimate public-order objectives, and that they are subject to legality control by the prefect and judicial review before administrative courts. It further invokes measures adopted to improve the housing situation of vulnerable groups, including budget allocations, increased funding for outreach and day centres, an increase in the allocation of social housing to homeless households, among others. 50. The complainant organisations dispute the Government’s argument regarding the effectiveness of judicial review and note that such decrees continue to be adopted despite occasional reversals in court. 51. As regards the Government’s references to policies aimed at combating homelessness, the Committee notes that the present complaint focuses on whether the proliferation of municipal decrees in question complies with Article 30, alone and read in conjunction with Article E of the Charter. While such general social-policy measures are relevant as contextual background, they lie outside the scope of the specific allegations as framed by the complainant organisations. The Committee further considers that the existence and effectiveness of remedies to challenge the municipal decrees in question constitute a distinct allegation under Article 30 of the Charter, which must be examined separately. 52. Regarding the alleged violations of Articles 11 and 31§1 of the Charter, these concern the effects of the municipal decrees in terms of restricting the ability of those concerned to enjoy the right to protection of health and the right to housing. However, the Committee notes that the complaint did not provide sufficiently detailed or specific information to enable a separate assessment under these provisions. The Committee considers that Article 30 of the Charter provides a more appropriate framework for examining the broader implications of the measures in question, including in connection with healthcare or housing. The Committee has already emphasized the very close link between the effectiveness of the right recognized by Article 30 of the Charter and the enjoyment of the rights recognized by other provisions, including the right to protection of health (Article 11) and the right to housing (Article 31) (Conclusions 2013, Statement of interpretation on Article 30). 53. Accordingly, the Committee will address the allegations contained in the complaint as follows: - alleged violation of Article 30 of the Charter as regards the proliferation of municipal decrees on begging; - alleged violation of Article 30 of the Charter as regards the existence of remedies; - alleged violation of Article E read in conjunction with Article 30 of the Charter as regards the proliferation of municipal decrees on begging. 54. The Committee notes that the complainant organisations refer to the persons affected by municipal decrees variously as “socially and economically vulnerable people,” “homeless people,” “people living on the street,” “people in a situation of hardship,” “people in need and in great distress,” among other terms. For the purposes of this decision, the Committee considers that they are “people living in poverty” and will, as a rule, use this term accordingly. It also observes that most of these individuals are likely to be homeless. In this respect, the Committee recalls its definition of “homeless persons” as those who do not legally have at their disposal a dwelling or other form of adequate housing within the meaning of Article 31§1 of the Charter (Conference of European Churches (CEC) v. Netherlands, Complaint No. 90/2013, decision on the merits of 1 July 2014, §135). In the French context, it further notes the national definition of homelessness, which includes, besides people sleeping rough or unsheltered, people living in non-conventional shelters, as well as those living in various types of State-funded emergency or temporary accommodation, such as shelters, accommodation for migrants, gymnasiums, or hotels (see § 75 below). I. ALLEGED VIOLATIONS OF ARTICLE 30 OF THE CHARTER 55. Article 30 of the Charter reads as follows: Article 30: The right to protection against poverty and social exclusion Part I: “Everyone has the right to protection against poverty and social exclusion.” Part II: “With a view to ensuring the effective exercise of the right to protection against poverty and social exclusion, the Parties undertake: a. to take measures within the framework of an overall and co-ordinated approach to promote the effective access of persons who live or risk living in a situation of social exclusion or poverty, as well as their families, to, in particular, employment, housing, training, education, culture and social and medical assistance; b. to review these measures with a view to their adaptation if necessary.” As to the municipal decrees on begging A – Arguments of the parties 1. The complainant organisations 56. The complainant organisations state that, according to official estimates, there were approximately 300,000 people in a situation of homelessness in France in 2019, including some 40,000 rough sleepers, a figure which, according to the Court of Audit, had increased by 10% since 2012. They submit that homelessness disproportionately affects persons leaving child protection care, former prisoners, and persons discharged from psychiatric institutions, as well as people experiencing family breakdown or those with addictions. They also refer to repeated evictions from shanty towns and, in the case of Travellers, from unauthorised halting areas, as a major cause of homelessness. The complainant organisations emphasise that such evictions often take place without notice and without any provision for relocation or rehousing. They further contend that the supply of emergency accommodation does not meet demand, and that existing shelters may be inadequate due to sub-standard hygiene and safety conditions, or unreasonable requirements such as refusing access to those with pets, those with addictions, or to family and friends. The complainant organisations maintain that, due to insufficient housing provision, many people are forced to live on the street either temporarily or permanently. 57. Against this background, the complainant organisations allege that numerous municipalities have adopted decrees banning begging and other forms of conduct typically engaged in by people living in poverty in public spaces, such as sitting or lying in certain areas, remaining in public places for prolonged periods, gathering in groups, or being accompanied by dogs. They maintain that these measures deprive people living in poverty of the means to meet their basic needs, thereby undermining their human dignity. Moreover, the decrees in question are often motivated not by genuine threats to public order, but rather by economic considerations, or public discomfort with visible poverty, which they argue are not legitimate grounds for restricting human rights. 58. The complainant organisations further assert that anti-begging measures are informed and reinforced by negative stereotypes about people living in poverty, who are often regarded as violent, rough, dirty, inebriated, and criminal by nature. They refer to the National Agency for Active Solidarity, which reported that homeless people are often regarded as “unable to live in a home”, a prejudicial view hindering their access to the “Housing First” programme. They also cite allocation data indicating that homeless applicants are significantly less likely to obtain social housing than other eligible groups such as tenants or people in temporary accommodation. They argue that, by displacing people living in poverty from urban centres, law-enforcement authorities break social bonds and disrupt the provision of essential services. As an example, they refer to an order dated 9 October 2023 by the Paris Police Prefect prohibiting charities from organising food distribution outlets within a defined urban perimeter on the grounds of public-order concerns linked to gatherings of poor people. On 17 October 2023, an administrative court suspended the decree, finding that the alleged risk to public order was unfounded and that it restricted access to food for hundreds of people in a situation of severe poverty. The complainant organisations submit that dispersal measures also increase the vulnerability of those affected to crime, such as theft or assault. Local authorities prioritise law enforcement to the detriment of measures that address the root causes of homelessness and poverty. They note that most anti-begging decrees do not provide for any referral to shelters or social support services. The complainant organisations further refer to the case of Lacatus v. Switzerland, whereby the ECtHR found a violation of the ECHR in circumstances allegedly like those in the present case. 59. The complainant organisations dispute the notion of “aggressive begging,” noting that the mere presence of people begging in public is often regarded as intrusive and disagreeable. They contend that the underlying public-order concerns are overstated and that the wording of the decrees is vague, thereby granting broad discretion to municipal police. They argue that enforcement results in people living in poverty being physically excluded from urban centres and prevents them from seeking public assistance. 60. The complainant organisations further submit that the administrative courts’ scrutiny of the decrees in question is inconsistent. Specifically, while some courts annul them on grounds of necessity and proportionality, others uphold them almost by default. 61. To illustrate the scope of the problem, the complainant organisations provided a sample of 95 anti-begging decrees issued in 51 municipalities between 2008 and 2024, together with numerous police reports (procès-verbaux) and logs (mains courantes) from several municipalities. They also note the absence of official records on the total number of such decrees, and point out that the large number of municipalities in France (about 34,900) makes systematic monitoring difficult. 2. The respondent Government 62. The Government submits that the mayors’ police powers are strictly regulated and may be exercised only to maintain public order. Moreover, any measures must be proportionate to the aim pursued. It stresses that municipal decrees may be challenged before the courts, which apply rigorous judicial review. The Government notes that the standing rules before such courts are permissive, enabling associations and affected individuals to bring proceedings. The Government also distinguishes between the preventive role of the administrative police and the punitive function of the judicial police. 63. The Government denies that municipal decrees target people in a situation of homelessness or poverty and submits that any restrictions are tailored to the local circumstances, such as seasonal variations in population or specific recurring public-safety concerns, and that decrees are often limited in both time and space. It stresses that municipal decrees are not instruments for allocating social support, which is a function performed by other authorities. In this regard, the Government refers to: the right to a formal address (“domiciliation”) that enables homeless persons or residents of informal settlements to access social benefits and exercise other rights; the unconditional right to emergency accommodation for all persons in need, including those without a valid residency permit, under Art. L345-2-2 of the Social Action and Family Code; the Five-Year Plan for Housing First and the Fight against Homelessness (2018–2022), and the follow-up Housing First Plan; the enforceable right to housing known as the DALO Act; and the enforceable right to emergency accommodation under Art. L441-2-3-1 of the Building and Housing Code. In the field of healthcare provision for persons living in poverty, the Government cites the deployment of mixed mobile teams combining health and social professionals, the provision of on-site healthcare services in shelters, extending access to health insurance, subject to eligibility conditions, and providing personalised support with a view to ensuring effective access to healthcare. 64. The Government seeks to distinguish the ECtHR Lacatus v. Switzerland judgment on the basis that France, unlike Switzerland, does not have a blanket ban on begging in place and that any restrictions on begging must be proportionate to the aim pursued. The Government maintains further that municipal decrees relating to begging do not interfere with the right of those concerned to seek and retain an occupation. B – Assessment of the Committee 65. The Committee recalls that the scope of Article 30 relates both to protection against poverty, understood as involving situations of social precarity, as well as to protection against social exclusion, understood as involving obstacles to inclusion and citizen participation, either in an autonomous manner or in combination with other provisions of the Charter (Conclusions 2013, Statement of interpretation on Article 30). In a recent case concerning the living conditions in a shanty town, the Committee considered that the stigmatisation and discriminatory messages directed against its inhabitants, including public statements associating them with criminality and drug dealing, exacerbated their social exclusion and were indicative of a violation of Article 30 of the Charter (Defence for Children International (DCI) and Others v. Spain, Complaint No. 206/2022, decision of 11 September 2024, §§ 206-207). 66. Article 30 of the Charter obliges States Parties to implement an overall and coordinated approach to combating poverty and social exclusion (Conclusions 2003, France). This approach should consist of an analytical framework, a set of priorities, and corresponding measures to remove obstacles to access to social rights, in particular employment, housing, training, education, culture, and social and medical assistance. It must link and integrate public policies in a consistent way, embedding the fight against poverty and social exclusion in all strands of policy and moving beyond a purely sectoral or target-group approach. Effective coordination mechanisms should exist at all levels, including at the level of delivery of assistance and services to the end-users (Statement on Covid-19 and social rights, 24 March 2021). 67. Any restriction on the enjoyment of rights protected by the Charter must comply with the conditions laid down in Article G of the Charter (Statement on Covid-19 and social rights, 24 March 2021; Greek General Confederation of Labour (GSEE) v. Greece, Complaint No. 111/2014, decision on the merits of 23 March 2017, §83). Thus, any restriction must (i) be prescribed by law, satisfying the requirement of precision and foreseeability; (ii) pursue a legitimate purpose, i.e. the protection of the rights and freedoms of others, the public interest, national security, public health, or morals; and (iii) be necessary in a democratic society for the pursuance of these purposes, i.e. there must be a reasonable relationship of proportionality between the restriction on a right and the legitimate aim(s) pursued. In this last respect, any restriction should respond to a pressing social need and be the least restrictive measure in terms of its impact on Charter rights, among those likely to achieve the aim pursued. Restrictions under Article G must be interpreted narrowly (GSEE v. Greece, op. cit., §83). Whether there was a restriction 68. The complainant organisations submit that the municipal decrees on begging exacerbate the situation of persons living in poverty by depriving them of the ability to seek public assistance, penalising them for engaging in life-sustaining conduct, and by disrupting the provision of essential services, among other effects. The evidence before the Committee shows that persons living in poverty are routinely fined and removed from urban centres for conduct including begging, occupying public spaces for prolonged periods, congregating in groups with or without dogs, misusing communal facilities, consuming alcohol, playing music loudly, or urinating in public (see § 19 above). The Committee considers that such measures constitute a restriction on the right to protection against poverty and social exclusion within the meaning of Article 30 of the Charter. Whether the restriction was prescribed by law 69. The Committee notes that the municipal decrees on begging give effect to the mayors’ police powers, in accordance with Art. L. 2212-2 of the General Code on Local and Regional Authorities and supplemented by Conseil d'État in its jurisprudence (see §§ 13, 23-34 above). The Committee also notes the complainants’ argument that key notions such as “public order” are vague and unforeseeable in their application. The Committee acknowledges the formal existence of a legal basis and will examine the allegations of vagueness and lack of foreseeability in its proportionality assessment (see §§ 78-79 below). Whether the restriction pursued a legitimate aim 70. The Government submits that the municipal decrees on begging are intended to protect public order, within the meaning of Art. L. 2212-1 of the General Code on Local and Regional Authorities. For their part, the complainant organisations maintain that begging does not in itself disturb public order and that the impugned decrees address manifestations of poverty rather than conduct posing an actual threat to public safety or tranquillity. The Committee acknowledges that national authorities may have a valid interest in regulating certain narrowly defined forms of begging, for example, where there is substantiated evidence of human trafficking, exploitation of vulnerable persons, or conduct involving threats, coercion, or other forms of aggression (see mutatis mutandis, Lacatus v. Switzerland, op. cit., § 97). Therefore, the restriction in question pursues prima facie legitimate aims within the meaning of Article G, namely the protection of the rights and freedoms of others. Whether the restriction was necessary in a democratic society 71. The Committee will examine first whether the reasons adduced to justify the need for municipal decrees were relevant and sufficient. The Committee notes that local authorities often invoke economic considerations to justify anti-begging measures, whether in terms of improving the tourist experience, or protecting the viability of local businesses, which are allegedly threatened by a large influx of people living in poverty occupying urban centres. In agreement with the ECtHR and other international monitoring bodies having expressed views on the matter, the Committee considers that economic arguments of this nature cannot serve to justify restrictions on the enjoyment of human rights (see mutatis mutandis Lacatus v. Switzerland, op. cit., §113, and the sources cited therein). 72. The parties disagree as to whether penalising begging and other conduct typically resorted to by people living in poverty in public spaces is capable of protecting public order (see § 70 above). Having regard to the documentation submitted as evidence (see § 61 above), the Committee observes that law enforcement interventions are generally triggered by people being present in public spaces and engaging in conduct such as begging, sleeping, congregating with others, or consuming alcohol, without there being objective indications of an actual public order risk. The Committee notes further that administrative courts routinely annul municipal decrees on grounds of lack of necessity or proportionality. By way of example, in a judgment dated 5 April 2022 (TA Nice, No. 1903151), the Nice Administrative Tribunal held that "most [evidence submitted by the State] merely documents the simple presence of homeless people in public spaces or begging, without any resistance or violence on their part toward the municipal police or passers-by and rarely accompanied by dogs." The Committee also notes that the Government has not adduced any evidence to demonstrate that begging and other conduct adopted by people living in poverty in public spaces as such are in breach of public order. 73. The Committee notes that local authorities routinely cite the high number of complaints received from traders, residents, and passers-by as evidence of a threat to public order, justifying the adoption of the prohibitions in question (see § 21 above). The Committee observes that certain decrees also refer to “feelings of insecurity” in connection with the presence of people living in poverty in public spaces. In the Committee’s view, such perceptions are more indicative of public prejudice towards people who engage in begging or sleeping rough than of an actual danger to public order. The Committee notes, with reference to the Guiding Principles on Extreme Poverty and Human Rights, that “persons living in poverty have a right to be protected from the negative stigma attached to conditions of poverty” (§ 21). Common manifestations of stigma include widely held beliefs, such as those suggesting that people engage in begging because it is lucrative or because they do not want to work, or that people living in poverty are inherently dangerous, dishonest, or incapable of raising children. In turn, stigma resulting from behaviours such as begging or sleeping rough may lead those concerned to experience verbal or physical abuse in their daily life (on the issue of “anti-poor prejudice,” see UN Special Rapporteur on Extreme Poverty and Human Rights, Banning discrimination on grounds of socioeconomic disadvantage: an essential tool in the fight against poverty, U.N. Doc. A/77/157, 13 July 2022, §§ 5-6 and the sources cited therein). The Committee emphasizes that, contrary to the punitive approach adopted in the present case, Article 30 of the Charter requires that national authorities take appropriate measures to foster community awareness about homelessness, built on empathy and understanding. 74. The Committee’s proportionality analysis next examines the effects of municipal decrees on people living in poverty. The Committee considers that begging, in many cases, constitutes a means of survival for the sizable number of people living in poverty in France (see mutatis mutandis Lacatus v. Switzerland, noting that the applicant “had a right, inherent in human dignity, to be able to convey her plight and attempt to meet her basic needs through begging,” op. cit., § 107). 75. The Committee notes, in this context, that, according to Eurostat, the share of people at risk of poverty or social exclusion in France was 15.9% in 2023, compared to 15.6% in 2015, whereas the share of people suffering from severe material and social deprivation was 12.6% in 2023, compared to 14.7% in 2015. According to the National Institute of Statistics (INSEE), the share of people living below the monetary poverty threshold in Metropolitan France was of 14,4%, or 9.1 million people in 2022, compared to a 13.7% share in 2016. According to the most recent official data, between 2001 and 2012, overall homelessness increased by 58% (from 93,000 to 141,500 persons), while the number of children living in homelessness increased by 85% (Bénédicte Mordier, “Introduction de cadrage: les sans-domicile en France – caractéristiques et principales évolutions entre 2001 et 2012”, Economie et Statistique, Nos. 488–489, 2016). According to a more recent estimate, 350,000 people were in a situation of homelessness (“sans domicile”) in 2024, representing a 145% increase on 2012 (Fondation Abbé Pierre, “30e rapport sur l'état du mal-logement en France”, 2025, p. 315 et seq.). This estimate is based on the definition of homelessness used by the INSEE, which includes those who are unsheltered and those living in state-funded emergency accommodation. According to the same source, 2.7 million households were registered on waiting lists for social housing in 2024 and 19,000 households were subjected to forced evictions. 76. The Committee also notes that municipal decrees authorise the police to systematically remove people living in poverty from urban centres, thereby depriving them of one of the last means available to meet their basic needs. While those concerned retain the theoretical possibility of continuing to beg outside those areas, the lack of foot traffic on the outskirts of towns would render such activity pointless from their perspective. The Committee further questions the effectiveness of imposing fines on people who lack the means to pay them. Moreover, having regard to the documentation submitted as evidence (see § 61 above), the Committee notes that law enforcement also interferes with social relationships and daily routines in a manner that infringes the autonomy of people living in poverty, disrupts the provision of essential services, such as food distribution, shelter, healthcare, or substance-abuse treatment, may force them into more harmful situations, exacerbates stigma against them, generates mistrust in the state, and causes or reinforces trauma. The Committee reiterates that living in a situation of poverty and social exclusion as such violates the dignity of human beings (Conclusions 2013, Statement of interpretation on Article 30). Insofar as municipal decrees compound the disadvantage of people who must rely on public spaces for their survival, by entrenching their social exclusion and marginalisation, they may be said to amount to “double victimisation”. 77. The Committee also assesses whether the aim of protecting public order could be achieved by measures less restrictive of the rights of those concerned. In this regard, the Committee addresses, first, the question of whether more narrowly focused municipal decrees on begging could be adopted and, second, the reliance of local authorities on punitive law-enforcement approaches to address visible poverty. 78. Firstly, the Government contends that the prohibitions contained in municipal decrees are circumscribed, have geographical, temporal, and material limitations, such that they cannot be characterised as a blanket ban on begging. The complainant organisations, however, maintain that the regulations in question are couched in language that is vague and unforeseeable in its application and that, in practice, they impose sweeping prohibitions on begging and other specified conduct. The Committee also finds it relevant in this context that the ECtHR has deemed the scope of national regulations (such as a blanket ban in Switzerland or a qualified ban in Denmark) to be a key factor in determining whether there has been a violation of the ECHR in connection with the enforcement of begging bans in individual cases (see §§ 25-26 above). 79. The Committee notes that municipal decrees are replete with notions such as “prolonged occupation”, “aggressive begging,” or “public order,” which lack a clear definition. The Committee further refers to its findings elsewhere in the present decision concerning the authorities’ loose enforcement practices (see § 72 above), the line of national jurisprudence invalidating municipal decrees on the ground that the underlying public-order concerns are unsubstantiated or of a lack of compliance with the Conseil d'État’s guidance by local authorities (see § 90 below), and the stark imbalance of power between police agents and people living on the street. In light of these factors, the Committee concludes that the municipal decrees in question confer arbitrary discretion on law-enforcement authorities in determining what conduct will attract sanction, akin to a blanket ban. 80. The Committee has already acknowledged that national authorities may have a valid interest in restricting certain forms of begging (see § 70 above), for example, where human trafficking or aggressive behaviour is involved. The Criminal Code, which includes provisions such as “exploitation of begging,” “extortion,” “endangering minors,” and “human trafficking,” already provides a legal basis for criminalising such conduct. Furthermore, the authorities have at their disposal other regulations that do not directly target people living in poverty in public places, enabling them to restrict certain behaviours on public-health or public-order grounds. For example, the Public Health Code allows law enforcement authorities to take action against “public and manifest drunkenness” (see § 16 above). 81. Secondly, the Committee notes that national authorities have unquestionably taken measures with a view to reducing poverty and social exclusion, backed up by substantial public expenditure, pursuant to their obligations under Article 30 of the Charter (see § 63 above). At the same time, the Committee refers to its conclusion above that the ongoing reliance on a punitive law-enforcement approach as a frontline response to homelessness and poverty is counterproductive, as it exacerbates the situation of people living in poverty (see § 76 above). The Committee considers that law enforcement is not exempt from the requirement under Article 30 that action be taken “within the framework of an overall and coordinated approach to combating poverty and social exclusion” (see § 66 above). On the contrary, local police forces must develop partnerships with social service providers to address more efficiently the complex needs of people living in poverty in public spaces and facilitate access to essential services, based on working methods that are rights-based, trauma-informed, and that foster mutual trust between police and the affected communities. 82. The Committee notes further that there is a growing international consensus around the position that penalising life-sustaining activities in public spaces violates human rights. At the global level, this consensus is reflected, among other documents, in the UN Guiding Principles on Extreme Poverty and Human Rights and Guidelines on the Implementation of the Right to Adequate Housing (see §§ 39-42 above). At the regional level, the ECtHR has found that blanket bans on begging violate the ECHR, particularly when begging is the only option to ensure survival (see § 25 above). The African Commission on Human and Peoples’ Rights has adopted Principles on the Decriminalization of Petty Offences, and the African Court on Human and Peoples’ Rights has issued an advisory opinion on vagrancy laws (see § 45-46 above), advancing similar views. Lastly, the Inter-American Commission on Human Rights has emphasised that bans on begging and other conduct deemed “undesirable” or contrary to public order aggravated the situation of exclusion, disadvantage, and discrimination faced by persons living in poverty (see § 47 above). 83. In light of the above considerations, the Committee finds that the municipal decrees in question do not serve to advance the legitimate aim of protecting of the rights and freedoms of others. On the contrary, by adopting such measures, local authorities perpetuate and exacerbate the situation of people living in poverty in public spaces and reinforce stigma and prejudice against them. Given the vagueness of the language used and the broad scope of their application, these provisions are liable to being abused in individual cases. The Committee therefore holds that there is a violation of Article 30 of the Charter in this regard. As to the existence of effective remedies A – Arguments of the parties 1. The complainant organisations 84. The complainant organisations submit that local authorities often adopt decrees in manifest breach of the criteria laid down by the Conseil d'État in its jurisprudence, providing several examples to that effect. They attribute this persistence to the low risk of an appeal being filed. They state that, to their knowledge, there has been only one case of a prefect challenging a decree of this type pursuant to Art. L. 2131-8 of the General Code on Local and Regional Authorities. Namely, in 2022, the Prefect of Moselle intervened in this manner to prevent the Mayor of Metz from issuing repetitive anti-begging decrees, even after an administrative court found them to be in breach of the relevant regulations. The complainant organisations further maintain that numerous such decrees are issued across France each year, making systematic monitoring and challenge impossible in practice. They also note that the persons directly affected, predominantly homeless or otherwise living in extreme poverty, face substantial barriers to initiating legal proceedings themselves, including lack of legal representation, illiteracy, limited education, discrimination, and difficulty navigating complex legal and administrative procedures. As regards the length of administrative proceedings, they contend that anti-begging decrees typically apply for several months, while the requests for suspension are rarely granted in practice, due to particularly onerous procedural requirements. Thus, in most cases, court decisions are delivered after the decrees in question have already lapsed, having produced their intended effects. 2. The respondent Government 85. The Government maintains that municipal decrees may be appealed before administrative courts, taking advantage of permissive standing requirements and rigorous judicial review, with reference to Art. L. 2131-8 of the General Code on Local and Regional Authorities (see § 14 above). According to the Government, this is an accessible remedy, with claimants being exempt from court fees and from having to secure legal representation. The Government notes that prefects are responsible for ensuring the legality of such decrees and, where appropriate, initiating proceedings to challenge them. It further submits that the length of proceedings is not excessive, referring to 2002 statistics suggesting an average length of 16 months and eight days before administrative tribunals, 11 months and 28 days before administrative courts of appeal, and 11 months and 17 days before the Conseil d'État. The Government also submits several administrative court judgments showing that NGOs have successfully secured the invalidation of local police decrees by having recourse to this remedy. B – Assessment of the Committee 86. The Committee recalls that, while the Charter does not contain an express provision on “access to justice,” this concept is embedded within its various provisions and has been clarified through the Committee’s case-law and interpretative statements. In particular, the Committee has previously held that the existence of an “independent body” for the purposes of ensuring an effective remedy constitutes an essential component of ensuring respect for the rights enshrined in the Charter (see for example Norwegian Association of Small and Medium Enterprises (SMB Norge) v. Norway, Complaint No. 198/2021, and Fellesforbundet for Sjøfolk (FFFS) v. Norway, Complaint No. 209/2022, decision on the merits of 13 September 2023, §§ 62-67). The Committee considers that Article 30 similarly incorporates a key access-to-justice component, requiring that those who allege a violation of the right to protection against poverty and social exclusion must have at their disposal a remedy that is effective both in law and in practice. 87. In the present case, the Committee notes that the parties have not submitted any information on the options available to those concerned to challenge law enforcement action pursuant to municipal decrees in individual cases. This would be pertinent information, having regard, as discussed above, to the wide degree of discretion enjoyed by law enforcement authorities (see §79 above), as well as the range of limitations faced by those targeted, including in connection with literacy, education, poverty, discrimination, access to legal representation and the ability to navigate complex legal and administrative systems. 88. The Committee refers to the parties’ submissions that abstract administrative review is, in practice, the only available remedy for challenging municipal decrees. While the Government maintains that this is an effective remedy, the complainant organisations contend that that is not the case, inter alia, due to the length of proceedings, and its limited dissuasive effect in preventing repetition. The Committee also notes the complainant organisations’ claim, undisputed by the Government, that administrative proceedings often result in the invalidation of the decrees in question. 89. The Committee accepts the Government’s submission that standing requirements are sufficiently broad, and that access is not hindered by requirements such as court fees or mandatory legal representation. However, the same cannot be said of the manner in which prefects discharge their role in this context. While the Government emphasizes that prefects are charged with filtering out any decrees that raise legality concerns and initiating administrative proceedings to challenge them, the complainant organisations submit that prefects have only ever challenged one such decree. The Committee further notes that those directly affected by municipal decrees are unlikely to be in a position to challenge them due to systematic obstacles, as discussed above (see § 87 above). It follows that NGOs have a key role in ensuring oversight in this context, as also acknowledged by the parties. 90. In practice, however, this remedy is unlikely to be brought to bear, due to the shortage of resources in the NGO sector, especially given the size of the task, which involves identifying and challenging hundreds of decrees every year. Moreover, even where administrative proceedings are successfully initiated and result in invalidation, a decision is likely to be handed down after the decree in question has already expired and produced its effects. At the same time, requests for suspension are unlikely to succeed due to strict evidentiary requirements. Lastly, the Committee notes that the prospect of invalidation does not have a sufficient dissuasive effect on local authorities. The complainant organisations provide examples of decrees issued as recently as 2023 that appear to violate principles established by the Conseil d'État in its jurisprudence. For example, a decree issued in Mulhouse in 2022 banned begging in multiple locations in the town centre for twelve hours a day, year-round, on a permanent basis, and even in the absence a notional risk to public order. The complainant organisations also referred to the practice in certain municipalities of continuing to issue substantively similar decrees in defiance of administrative court invalidation decisions. The Committee points out that the practice at the local level of issuing such police decrees has continued largely unabated since begging was decriminalised in 1994, despite Conseil d'État and ordinary administrative court decisions regularly rebuking local authorities for falling foul of the law. 91. The Committee therefore considers that the remedies available to persons affected by municipal decrees are not effective in law and in practice. It accordingly holds that there is a violation of Article 30 of the Charter, on the basis that people affected by municipal decrees do not have an effective remedy for challenging the violation of the right to protection against poverty and social exclusion. II. ALLEGED VIOLATION OF ARTICLE E READ IN CONJUNCTION WITH ARTICLE 30 OF THE CHARTER 92. Article 30 and Article E of the Charter read as follows: Article 30 – The right to protection against poverty and social exclusion Part I: “Everyone has the right to protection against poverty and social exclusion." Part II: “With a view to ensuring the effective exercise of the right to protection against poverty and social exclusion, the Parties undertake: a. to take measures within the framework of an overall and co-ordinated approach to promote the effective access of persons who live or risk living in a situation of social exclusion or poverty, as well as their families, to, in particular, employment, housing, training, education, culture and social and medical assistance; b. to review these measures with a view to their adaptation if necessary." E – Non-discrimination “The enjoyment of the rights set forth in this Charter shall be secured without discrimination on any ground such as race, colour, sex, language, religion, political or other opinion, national extraction or social origin, health, association with a national minority, birth or other status.” A – Arguments of the parties 1. The complainant organisations 93. The complainant organisations submit that municipal decrees on begging discriminate on the ground of socio-economic status, as they prohibit conduct that is more likely to be engaged in by people living in poverty, in particular people living in homelessness. They argue that the prohibited conduct, such as begging, prolonged presence or “occupation” of public space, sitting or lying down in public areas, gathering in groups, being accompanied by dogs, consuming alcohol, rummaging through bins, or sleeping in public, is conduct intrinsically linked to living in poverty and homelessness. They also refer to the language used in these decrees, as well as in certain public statements by local authorities, and police reports submitted as evidence, suggesting that people are targeted based on their socio-economic status. For instance, a sample of police reports from Nice and Metz shows that most law enforcement interactions with the street population involved people experiencing homelessness. The complainant organisations add that people living in poverty in public spaces are treated with suspicion and subjected to persistent monitoring and surveillance. 2. The respondent Government 94. The Government denies that the decrees in question give rise to differential treatment within the meaning of Article E of the Charter.
