In its view, local authorities are permitted to issue decrees that prohibit conduct for the purposes of protecting public order and are limited in time and space. Conversely, local authorities are not entitled to use their police powers to target certain groups under the guise of protecting public order, as this would constitute an abuse of power. B – Assessment of the Committee 95. The Committee recalls that discrimination is defined as a difference in treatment between persons in comparable situations where it does not pursue a legitimate aim, is not based on objective and reasonable grounds or is not proportionate to the aim pursued (Syndicat national des Professions du tourisme v. France, Complaint No. 6/1999, decision on the merits of 10 October 2000, §25). Article E not only prohibits direct discrimination, but also all forms of indirect discrimination that may arise by failing to take due and positive account of all relevant differences or by failing to take adequate steps to ensure that the rights and collective advantages that are open to all are genuinely accessible by and to all (Associazione sindacale "La Voce dei Giusti" v. Italy, Complaint No. 105/2014, decision on the merits of 18 October 2016, §63). The grounds of prohibited discrimination listed in Article E of the Charter are not exhaustive and include poverty (Conclusions 2013, Statement of interpretation on Article 30) and socio-economic status (International Federation for Human Rights (FIDH) v. Ireland, Complaint No. 110/2014, decision on the merits of 12 May 2017, §124 and European Federation of National Organisations working with the Homeless (FEANTSA) v. Belgium, Complaint No. 203/2021, decision on the merits of 17 October 2024, §150). 96. The Committee notes that the municipal decrees in question routinely designate the target group, usually in the preambles, as people who are begging and/or are occupying public space for prolonged periods, on the basis that they constitute a risk to public order. Moreover, the decrees penalise life-sustaining conduct and conduct inherently linked to poverty and homelessness, such as begging, occupying public spaces for prolonged periods, congregating in groups with or without dogs, misusing communal facilities, consuming alcohol, playing music loudly, or urinating in public (see § 19 above). This is conduct that those concerned cannot reasonably avoid and which operates as a proxy for the status of being homeless. In other words, the regulations in question single out for punishment conduct that is inextricably linked to, and effectively defines, the status of being homeless. 97. In this context, the Committee notes that several international human rights monitoring bodies have expressed concern regarding the situation in France in relation to the right to housing, including the shortage of affordable and social housing, the shortage of emergency accommodation, the prevalence of forced evictions without adequate rehousing alternatives, the high and increasing number of persons experiencing homelessness, the high number of informal settlements or the disparate impact on vulnerable groups (see ECRI, §§ 30-31 above; the Special Rapporteur on adequate housing, § 35 above; and the ESCR Committee, § 36 above). The Committee also refers to a series of judgments finding France in violation of various provisions of the ECHR on grounds such as deficiencies in the implementation of the DALO Act or the forced eviction of Roma/Travellers without alternative housing solutions or considering their specific needs (see §27 above). 98. The Committee also notes that the municipal decrees in question are routinely enforced against people living in poverty in public spaces. Police reports submitted as evidence indicate that the individuals singled out for enforcement are homeless, usually referred to by the French acronym “SDS” (“sans domicile stable”, meaning “without stable housing”), and often describe their vulnerability in graphic terms, as illustrated by the following examples: - "I'm homeless, it's to get something to eat"; "I have no other way to feed myself"; "I'm hungry"; "I'm doing this to buy my medication"; "They are always asking us to leave"; "For the baby"; "Please, money for my family"; "I have nowhere to sleep, and I have to eat." - "The presence of four individuals and a dog, sleeping under the arcades"; "Two homeless people... spent the night under the arcades to protect themselves from the weather"; "A homeless person among garbage bins who is said to be making small fires to keep warm"; "He is homeless and no longer has access to his bank accounts. He wants housing and food"; "A homeless person who has been sleeping for several days on a bench in front of the hospital... he was notified... that he must leave the premises by Thursday morning at the latest so that the bench can be dismantled"; "A person showing signs of mental disability." 99. The Committee considers that legislation may be understood as discriminatory if it is based on generalised stereotypes and prejudices about a disadvantaged group. It has already been established above that the municipal decrees in question are not only based on prejudice but also exacerbate and perpetuate the stigmatisation of people living in poverty (see §§ 72-73 above). The Committee considers further that a punitive law enforcement approach on the basis of socio-economic status is counterproductive in two respects. First, the Committee doubts that, where people lack a home to go to, fines and removal orders can have a deterrent effect, reduce homelessness, or increase public safety. Second, and as discussed above, this approach is based on the myth that people in poverty are responsible for their situation. This preconceived idea misconstrues the nature of poverty, which is a complex phenomenon characterised by multiple and mutually reinforcing physical, economic, cultural, and social obstacles, from which it may be difficult to escape without assistance (see mutatis mutandis, Guiding Principles on Extreme Poverty and Human Rights, p. 2; see also §32 above). 100. In light of the above considerations, and insofar as the municipal decrees concerned introduce apparently neutral rules having a disproportionate impact on people living in poverty in public spaces, this constitutes indirect discrimination on the ground of socio-economic status. The Committee therefore holds that there is a violation of Article E read in conjunction with Article 30 of the Charter. CONCLUSION For these reasons, the Committee concludes: - unanimously that there is a violation of Article 30 of the Charter with respect to the proliferation of municipal decrees on begging; - unanimously that there is a violation of Article 30 of the Charter with respect to the absence of effective remedies; - unanimously that there is a violation of Article E read in conjunction with Article 30 of the Charter with respect to the existence of discrimination on the ground of socio-economic status. Tatiana PUIU Rapporteur George THEDOSIS Vice-President Henrik KRISTENSEN Executive Secretary
complainant: European Federation of National Organisations working with the Homeless (FEANTSA) and International Federation for Human Rights (FIDH)
